identity.title: Administration of Urban Planning and Community and Rural Development

lens.included_activities: No establishments qualify under the fixed lower-middle-market recurring outsourced-service lens. The NAICS population consists of government establishments administering and planning urban and rural development, including public zoning boards, commissions, and redevelopment agencies; their workflows are still assessed for AI and demand context.
lens.excluded_activities: All government agencies are excluded from the acquisition population because they are not transferable firms with commercial EBITDA; private planning, architecture, engineering, GIS, grant-administration, and consulting vendors are excluded because they are classified in other industries, as are captive units and non-control vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The in-code entities are tax- or grant-funded public administrations exercising governmental planning and zoning responsibilities rather than firms billing external customers for recurring services.

a.bridge: BLS describes urban and regional planners gathering and analyzing market, census, environmental, field, and GIS data; reviewing site plans; tracking regulation; preparing recommendations; and coordinating stakeholders. I map the data retrieval, document comparison, scenario analysis, drafting, case triage, and routine correspondence portions of that adjacent occupation to wage-weighted AI-addressable tasks in 925120 government establishments; the proxy may overstate addressability because BLS covers planners outside this NAICS and because public meetings, site inspection, discretionary judgment, and official decisions remain human.
a.rationale: Planning administration has a large information-processing surface, but stakeholder deliberation, field context, legal accountability, hearings, and approval or denial authority materially bound substitution.
a.change_evidence: A task-hour survey of municipal and regional planning departments, zoning boards, redevelopment agencies, and CDBG administrators, including current software automation, would materially change a.
caveats: The occupational source is broader than NAICS 925120 and includes private-sector planners.
Addressability varies with digitized records, GIS maturity, jurisdiction size, and state open-meeting and administrative-law requirements.

rho.rationale: Document intake, code lookup, staff-report drafting, public-comment summarization, grant reporting, and GIS-assisted screening can be implemented within five years, but procurement cycles, legacy systems, fragmented local government, records obligations, bias and due-process risk, explainability, and human approval slow operational adoption.
rho.change_evidence: A representative survey of local planning agencies reporting production AI deployment, percentage of cases processed, human override rates, procurement timelines, audit findings, and measured hours saved would materially change rho.
caveats: Federal AI governance evidence is only directional for state and local agencies.
Pilots and employee use do not establish workflow-level implementation or reliable substitution.

e.bridge: Census defines every establishment in 925120 as a government establishment administering and planning urban or rural development. Applying the fixed screen's requirement for LMM firms supplying recurring outsourced services therefore yields no eligible in-code firms; this exact transformation could only err upward if an operating private company were miscoded into the government NAICS.
e.rationale: Government zoning boards, commissions, redevelopment agencies, and program administrations are not commercial control-acquisition targets, while private contractors that support them belong to other NAICS codes.
e.change_evidence: Evidence from official establishment microdata that privately owned operating companies are validly classified in 925120 would change e.
caveats: Private vendors may serve the same workflows but are outside this industry code.
The frozen n input is missing, consistent with the absence of a commercial firm-count concept.

s5.bridge: Census identifies the complete in-code population as government establishments. Because a qualifying transfer is a control transfer of an eligible firm, reorganizations, elections, intergovernmental consolidations, and outsourcing procurements do not qualify; I therefore transform the government-only classification into zero qualifying control-transfer probability. Error would be upward only if a valid private in-code firm exists contrary to the industry definition.
s5.rationale: Public agencies do not undergo conventional acquisitions that transfer equity control of an operating LMM firm.
s5.change_evidence: Official evidence of a privately owned 925120 operating company and an observed control transaction would change s5.
caveats: This is a structural screen result rather than transaction-market measurement.
Privatization or outsourcing can create vendor opportunities but does not transfer control of the agency.

q.rationale: Commercial retention after contractual pass-through, renewal repricing, and price competition is not defined for tax- and grant-funded government establishments with no eligible firm population or customer billing model.
q.change_evidence: A valid population of privately owned 925120 firms with customer contracts, renewal terms, pricing data, and measured AI benefits would be required to estimate q.
caveats: Budget savings or increased public-service capacity are real benefits but are not the commercial retention construct defined by q.

d5.bridge: BLS projects urban and regional planner employment to rise 3% from 2024 to 2034 and attributes demand to demographic, transportation, environmental, housing, infrastructure, and resource-scarcity needs, while warning that government budgets constrain projects. I convert this broader occupation-level ten-year employment projection into a roughly half-horizon constant-quality service-demand ratio for government planning administration; the proxy may understate demand if AI productivity absorbs workload without headcount and may overstate it because private consulting employment is included.
d5.rationale: Population and housing change, infrastructure renewal, climate and environmental adaptation, grant administration, and land-use conflict sustain planning workload, offset by fiscal pressure, permitting reform, consolidation, and technology-enabled throughput.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year state and local planning-department workload forecasts covering applications, comprehensive-plan updates, zoning cases, grant programs, staffing, and real administrative spending would materially change d5.
caveats: Employment is an imperfect proxy for constant-quality service demand.
BLS projects the occupation across public and private industries rather than NAICS 925120 alone.

o.bridge: Census assigns administration and planning to government establishments, and HUD states that local governments administer CDBG and determine funded projects while consolidated planning requires jurisdictional assessment, annual action plans, and community-wide dialogue. I translate these institutional responsibilities into a high share of year-five work still requiring an accountable public operator even if software and consultants supply analysis; the proxy may overstate operator necessity where jurisdictions outsource more delivery and understate it where statutory decision authority is strictly nondelegable.
o.rationale: Software can automate analysis and private vendors can support delivery, but legitimate zoning, funding allocation, public participation, records, and approval decisions still require accountable governmental authority.
o.change_evidence: A jurisdiction-level study of fully outsourced or automated planning functions, distinguishing advisory work from legally reserved governmental decisions, would materially change o.
caveats: The remaining operator is a public agency, not a firm eligible under the acquisition lens.
State law determines which planning and zoning powers may be delegated.

narrative.executive_view: This code is structurally outside the rollup lens: Census defines it entirely as government establishments, so there are no qualifying firms or control transfers. The public workflows have substantial AI potential and durable demand, but those facts create vendor opportunities in other industries rather than acquisitions inside 925120.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate application intake, code and policy research, GIS-assisted screening, scenario analysis, staff-report drafting, public-comment summarization, grant reporting, and routine communication. Hearings, site context, stakeholder negotiation, due process, and approval authority continue to require accountable people.
narrative.value_capture: Public agencies may convert AI into shorter queues, greater analytical capacity, improved consistency, or lower administrative cost. There is no in-code commercial billing or ownership structure through which an acquirer could retain that gross benefit, so q is not defensibly defined.
narrative.firm_availability: No firm availability exists within the code under the fixed lens. Public reorganizations, intergovernmental transfers, vendor procurements, and privatized support contracts are not qualifying control transfers of a 925120 LMM company.
narrative.demand_durability: Housing needs, infrastructure, demographic change, environmental pressures, grants, zoning, and community participation support continuing administrative demand. Budgets, development cycles, permitting reform, consolidation, and productivity gains create downside to workload and staffing.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main uncertainty is not whether planning work persists, but how much can be automated safely across fragmented jurisdictions. For the acquisition screen, the decisive issue is classification rather than estimation: the frozen l and n are missing because this is government administration without receipts or a commercial firm population.
narrative.principal_driver: Data-heavy planning and grant-administration workflows provide a credible AI productivity surface with continuing public need.
narrative.principal_weakness: The NAICS population is government-only, leaving no eligible LMM firm, qualifying control transfer, or commercial benefit-retention model.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 925120 Administration of Urban Planning and Community and Rural Development
sources.supports: Defines the industry as government establishments administering and planning urban and rural development, including zoning boards and commissions.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Urban and Regional Planners
sources.supports: Planner duties, use of GIS and analytical software, government employment concentration, and 2024–2034 employment projection and demand drivers.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Projections and Worker Characteristics
sources.supports: Urban and regional planner employment of 44,700 in 2024 and projected employment of 46,200 in 2034.

sources.title: HUD Consolidated Planning
sources.supports: State and local assessment of housing and community-development needs, data-driven investment decisions, community dialogue, and annual action plans.

sources.title: HUD Community Development Block Grant Program
sources.supports: Annual grants to states, cities, and counties and the local-government role in administering programs and determining funded projects.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence: Federal Efforts Guided by Requirements and Advisory Groups
sources.supports: Government AI can improve agency operations but introduces risks and operates within extensive governance and oversight requirements.
